query: role of Acetyl CoA in HMG-CoA synthase, malic enzyme, malonyl CoA synthetase, fatty acid synthetase

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ketogenesis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Ketogenesis occurs primarily in the mitochondria of liver cells. Fatty acids are brought into the mitochondria via carnitine palmitoyltransferase (CPT-1) and then broken down into acetyl CoA via beta-oxidation. Two acetyl-CoA molecules are converted into acetoacetyl-CoA via the enzyme thiolase; this is also known as acetyl coenzyme A acetyltransferase (ACAT). Afterward, acetoacetyl-CoA is converted to HMG-CoA via the enzyme HMG-CoA synthase. HMG-CoA lyase then converts HMG-CoA to acetoacetate. Acetoacetate can be converted to either acetone through non-enzymatic decarboxylation or to beta-hydroxybutyrate via beta-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ketone Metabolism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Ketone synthesis in the liver produces acetoacetate and beta-hydroxybutyrate from two acetyl CoA molecules. This process begins in the mitochondria of the liver after transporting the fatty acyl CoA molecule into the inner mitochondrial membrane by the carnitine shuttle. The fatty acyl CoA molecules undergo beta-oxidation to become acetyl CoA molecules. Acetyl CoA molecules are either converted to malonyl CoA by acetyl CoA carboxylase or acetoacetyl CoA by 3-ketothiolase. Malonyl CoA serves as negative feedback to the liver CPT-1. Acetoacetyl CoA is further converted to 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl CoA (HMG CoA) by HMG CoA synthase. HMG CoA synthase is essential to this process, as it is the rate-limiting step for the synthesis of ketone bodies. HMG CoA synthase regulation is influenced positively by glucagon and negatively regulated by insulin. HMG CoA is finally converted to acetoacetate by HMG CoA lyase. At this point, acetoacetate may be converted to 3-B-hydroxybutyrate (3HB) by 3HB dehydrogenase. Acetoacetate and 3HB are organic acids that diffuse freely across cell membranes into the blood and other organs of the body.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fasting -- Mechanism -- Lipid
retrieved_docs.content: The process of ketogenesis first starts with the enzyme thiolase combining two molecules of acetyl-CoA into acetoacetyl-CoA. HMG-CoA synthase then adds another acetyl-CoA to create beta-hydroxy-beta-methylglutaryl-CoA. HMG-CoA lyase removes an acetyl-CoA group from the molecule to form acetoacetate. From this step, acetoacetate is broken down into acetone (by non-enzymatic decarboxylation) and beta-hydroxybutyrate (by D-beta-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase).

retrieved_docs.title: Bempedoic Acid -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Cholesterol synthesis is a complex multistep process in the endoplasmic reticulum of liver cells. This process starts with acetyl-CoA, the precursor for cholesterol synthesis. Acetyl-CoA is transported from mitochondria to the cytoplasm of the cell as citrate. When acetyl-CoA is inside the cytoplasm, adenosine triphosphate-citrate-lyase (ACL), a magnesium-dependent enzyme, releases acetyl-CoA from citrate. Thus, acetyl-CoA is an essential component in the cholesterol synthesis process.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastric Intrinsic Factor -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ado-Cbl is a cofactor for methylmalonyl CoA mutase enzyme. In this reaction, the methylmalonyl-CoA (a product from odd number fatty acid catabolism) converted to succinyl-CoA. The latter makes its way into two pathways: the citric acid cycle and heme synthesis. Accumulation of methylmalonyl-CoA responsible for: Neurological disorders due to the incorporation of methylmalonyl-CoA into the myelin sheath, and the result is neuron demyelination. Methylmalonic aciduria

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ketone Metabolism -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The important rate-limiting steps in ketone metabolism include hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL), acetyl CoA carboxylase, succinyl CoA-oxoacid transferase (SCOT), and HMG CoA synthase. HSL and HMG CoA synthase are inhibited by insulin and stimulated by glucagon. Acetyl CoA carboxylase is stimulated by insulin and inhibited by glucagon. All three of these enzymes have the same effect of slowing down ketone production in the presence of insulin and increasing it in the presence of glucagon. Finally, increased levels of acetoacetate in the mitochondria of target organs inhibit SCOT and therefore inhibit ketone metabolism. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ketogenesis -- Mechanism -- Regulation of Ketogenesis
retrieved_docs.content: Ketogenesis can be upregulated by hormones such as glucagon, cortisol, thyroid hormones, and catecholamines by causing a more significant breakdown of free fatty acids, thus increasing the amount available to be used in the ketogenic pathway. However, insulin is the primary hormonal regulator of this process. Insulin regulates many key enzymes in the ketogenic pathway, and a state of low insulin triggers the process. A low insulin state leads to: Increased free fatty acids (FFAs) Due to decreased inhibition of hormone-sensitive lipase Increased uptake of FFAs into the mitochondria Due to decreased activation of acetyl-CoA carboxylase, decreasing malonyl CoA, which disinhibits Carnitine Palmitoyltransferase 1 (CPT1) Increased production of ketone bodies Due to increased HMG-CoA activity

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gluconeogenesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Fatty acids are stored as triglycerides and mobilized by the hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL); glycerol from the triglyceride structure is released in blood to be taken up by the liver, phosphorylated by glycerol kinase, and oxidized into dihydroxyacetone phosphate -an intermediate of gluconeogenesis/ glycolysis pathway- by glycerol phosphate dehydrogenase. In contrast to the ketogenic even-chain fatty acids, Odd-chain fatty acids are converted into propionyl CoA during beta-oxidation. After several steps, propionyl CoA is converted into methylmalonyl CoA. Methylmalonyl CoA mutase/B12 catalyzes the conversion of the latter into succinyl-CoA. Succinyl-CoA is an intermediate of the TCA cycle that is eventually converted into oxaloacetic acid and enters the gluconeogenesis pathway. Even-chain fatty acids and purely ketogenic amino acids (leucine, lysine) converted to acetyl-CoA cannot enter gluconeogenesis because the step is catalyzed by pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) is irreversible. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cholesterol -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: While all cells can synthesize cholesterol to a small extent, the liver is the major site of cholesterol synthesis. The cholesterol synthesis occurs in the hepatic cytoplasm and requires enzymes n the cytoplasm and the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER). The first step of cholesterol biosynthesis involves condensing 2 molecules of acetyl-CoA to form acetoacetyl-CoA. Next, a cytosolic enzyme HMG-CoA synthase adds a third molecule of acetyl-CoA to acetoacetyl-CoA, making a six-carbon compound called 3-hydroxy-3-methyl glutaryl coenzyme A (HMG-CoA). An isozyme of HMG-CoA synthase in mitochondria catalyzes the rate-limiting reaction in ketogenesis. HMG-CoA reductase, a regulatory enzyme in the smooth ER catalyzes the next step, which reduces HMG-CoA to mevalonate. Synthesis of mevalonate is the key rate-limiting, committed step in the synthesis of cholesterol. A series of reactions convert Mevalonate to 3-isopentenyl pyrophosphate, farnesyl pyrophosphate, squalene, and lanosterol. Lanosterol then goes through another19-step ER-associated process to finally synthesize cholesterol. The terminal step is catalyzed by 7-dehydrocholesterol reductase that converts 7-dehydrocholesterol to cholesterol. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The inability to provide energy to tissues when glycogen stores are depleted secondary to MCADD results in a wide array of symptoms. Hypoketotic hypoglycemia with vomiting, progressing to seizures, and coma are typical presentations of this disease. MCADD is also thought to be responsible for a small portion of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) cases. This disease may become evident in early childhood, typically within the first 24 months of life, or remain asymptomatic until adulthood. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fasting -- Mechanism -- Lipid
retrieved_docs.content: Fatty acids are transformed into fatty acyl CoA through fatty acyl CoA synthetase. Energy from fatty acyl CoA is mainly produced through beta-oxidation and ketogenesis. Omega oxidation is a minor pathway that oxidizes fatty acids into dicarboxylic acids in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum. It remains a minor pathway unless mitochondrial beta-oxidation is defective. The location of beta-oxidation is dependent on the length of the fatty acid chain; short, medium, and long chains are degraded in the mitochondria while very-long and branched chains are degraded in peroxisomes. Every cycle of beta-oxidation produces 1 FADH, 1 NADH, and 1 acetyl CoA molecule. The very last cycle of produces 2 acetyl CoA (from even chained FA) or 1 acetyl CoA and 1 propionyl CoA (from odd chained FA).

retrieved_docs.title: Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: According to a recent study, the prevalence of acute symptomatic MCADD episodes was reduced from 0% to 13% from a pre-NBS rate of 66% to 95%. In the same study, the mortality associated with the initial episode was reduced to 3% to 4% from a pre-NBS rate of 14% to 21%. [27] In addition, this study reported a decrease in the prevalence and severity of cognitive impairment in patients with MCADD from 67% to 11% after the institution of expanded NBS in their cohort. The authors note that the higher prevalence of cognitive impairment in their study, both before and after NBS, when compared to other studies, is likely due to their inclusion of borderline and mild cognitive impairment. When restricting cognitive impairment to moderate and severe intellectual disability, they report results similar to previous studies, with a 13% prevalence before and 0% after NBS. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: HMG-CoA Reductase Inhibitors -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Conversion of 3-hydroxy-3-methyl glutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) to mevalonate by HMG-CoA reductase in the hepatocytes is the first and rate-limiting step in cholesterol biosynthesis. Statins competitively inhibit HMG-CoA reductase enzyme. Statins bind to the active site of the enzyme and induce a conformational change in its structure, thus reducing its activity. Also, the binding affinity of statins for HMG-CoA reductase is 10,000 times higher than the substrate (HMG-CoA), thus preventing the action of the enzyme and reducing the intracellular synthesis of cholesterol. Statins have a significant impact on lowering cholesterol since most of the circulating plasma cholesterol comes from the internal synthesis in hepatocytes rather than the diet. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Fruits, vegetables, and complex carbohydrates should be utilized to meet nutritional requirements.

retrieved_docs.title: Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Future Treatment Options
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrogen scavengers, eg, glycerol phenylbutyrate, are currently being studied as an adjunct therapy for patients with MCADD. Thus far, the studies have been restricted due to their sample size, limiting the extrapolation of results. Gene therapy using recombinant adenovirus vectors has shown promising results in animal models of MCADD, but human trials are still lacking. [1] Proteomics testing a kinetic model of a human liver with FAOD showed decreased pathway flux and low levels of free-mitochondrial CoA in symptomatic cases of MCADD. They predict that therapies that increase short-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase levels in patients with MCADD will increase pathway flux and free-mitochondrial CoA levels, which in their kinetic model correlates with a clinically asymptomatic state. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2083
retrieved_docs.content: Bempedoic acid is an oral lipid‑lowering medication that may treat فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني, a condition characterized by elevated blood lipids and increased cardiovascular risk. By inhibiting ATP‑citrate lyase upstream of HMG‑CoA reductase, bempedoic acid reduces hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to lower LDL‑cholesterol levels. This mechanism makes it a useful therapeutic option for patients with فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني, particularly those who are intolerant to statins or require additional lipid control. Effective management of this type of hyperlipidemia with bempedoic acid can help prevent atherosclerotic disease progression and improve overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 497
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise several specific agents that are members of this drug class. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin and pravastatin are all included within the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group, reflecting their shared mechanism of inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase to lower cholesterol levels. Recognizing these agents as members of the same therapeutic class aids clinicians in understanding their common pharmacologic effects and in selecting appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1847
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class that includes the individual agents atorvastatin, fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. All four medications share the same mechanism of action— inhibition of the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis. By blocking this enzyme, these agents lower low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and total cholesterol levels, thereby reducing the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and understand class‑related effects, dosing considerations, and potential adverse reactions such as myopathy or liver enzyme elevations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2060
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and pitavastatin are all members of the class HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents functions as lipid‑modifying therapy by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing cholesterol synthesis and helping to manage dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1624
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin are all members of the therapeutic class identified as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and reducing overall cardiovascular risk. Because they share the same mechanism of action, these statins are commonly used in the management of hyperlipidaemia and for primary or secondary prevention of atherosclerotic disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin belong to the same drug class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑related effects and potential drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin and pravastatin are both classified as members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels in patients. As members of the same therapeutic category, fluvastatin and pravastatin share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hyperlipidemia and reduce cardiovascular risk. Recognizing that both agents belong to the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor, plain lipid‑modifying drug class helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual patient factors while understanding their shared pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 190
retrieved_docs.content: chemistry, and the more accurate view is that pyridine‑based structures serve as scaffolds for various drugs, including certain statins, rather than being direct derivatives of torasemide. Overall, the knowledge base connects pharmacologic actions (β3‑adrenergic antagonism, HMG‑CoA reductase inhibition, sulfonylurea‑mediated insulin release) with specific chemical scaffolds (pyridine‑based cores) and clarifies that some reported hierarchical relationships are likely misclassifications. Understanding these interrelationships aids clinicians in recognizing how structural features influence drug class membership and therapeutic effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin and fluvastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, which leads to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As members of the same pharmacologic group, atorvastatin and fluvastatin share similar indications for treating hypercholesterolemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events, as well as comparable safety considerations such as the potential for myopathy and drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing both agents as part of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians apply class‑wide prescribing guidelines while selecting a specific statin based on potency, pharmacokinetic profile, and individual patient tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 848
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the therapeutic class known as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, a key step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and overall plasma lipid levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are commonly prescribed to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with hypercholesterolemia, atherosclerotic disease, or other dyslipidemias, and they form a cornerstone of lipid‑lowering therapy in contemporary medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2657
retrieved_docs.content: Rosuvastatin is classified as a member of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group, which are plain lipid‑modifying drugs used to lower cholesterol and manage dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, which are classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their inclusion in the plain lipid‑modifying drug category underscores their primary role in managing hyperlipidemia and preventing related cardiovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise a group of statin medications that share the same mechanism of action—blocking the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme to reduce cholesterol synthesis. The specific agents that belong to this class are fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. By lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, these statins are central to the management of hyperlipidaemia and the prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Understanding that fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑wide effects and potential adverse reactions.
